title: The room in the house where decisions get made
summary: Describe the room in a house where serious decisions actually happen, and why it is rarely the room a visitor would expect.
topic: The room where real family decisions happen
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about the specific room in a house where serious decisions get made, why it is that room and not another, and what this reveals about the family. Use the present perfect, a relative clause, a second conditional, and 'although' or 'however'.
hintStarters: In every house I have lived in, there has been one room in which real decisions actually get made, and in our family flat in Bangkok that room has never been…
If you visited my grandmother's house and tried to guess which corner of it had hosted the family's most consequential conversations, you would almost certainly point to the wrong one, because…
Although the formal living room is the space we keep for guests, what I have noticed over the years is that the room where decisions actually happen tends to be…

hintVocab.meaningVi: một không gian không chính thức

sampleText:
In every house I have lived in, there has been one room in which real decisions actually get made, and in our family flat in Bangkok that room has never been the formal living room. The living room is where guests are received, where the television sits, where my mother's wedding photograph hangs above the sofa. It is the room of polite welcomes and quiet birthdays. The actual decisions — about money, about whether my younger brother should move abroad for work, about my father's slow choice to retire two years earlier than he had planned — have all happened in the kitchen, at a small wooden table that seats four people comfortably and five only with difficulty. None of us decided this would be the rule. It is the rule because the kitchen does not flatter anyone, and a decision made in a flattering room often is not quite the decision you wanted to make.

If you visited my grandmother's house in the countryside and tried to guess which corner of it had hosted the family's most consequential conversations, you would almost certainly point to the wrong one. The visitor's instinct is to imagine the formal parlour, with its straight-backed chairs and its photographs of relatives in their best clothes. In fact, the parlour is where she serves tea to strangers and where my cousins quietly check the time. The real conversations happen on the back porch, sitting on plastic stools, looking out at a vegetable patch and a tin roof, while she shells beans into a metal bowl. Although the parlour was designed to look serious, the back porch is the place where it is possible to be honest, partly because the seating is uncomfortable and partly because the act of shelling beans gives both speakers something to look at when the conversation becomes difficult.

What I have noticed in many homes since then is that there is almost always a 'decision room' that is not the room a visitor would name. It tends to be slightly informal, slightly busy with another small activity, and slightly out of the way. The dining table where the homework also happens. The kitchen counter where someone is chopping vegetables. The car, on a long drive. Although families rarely choose this room consciously, I have come to think the choice is significant. A room that is too formal asks people to perform; a room that is too distracted does not let the conversation breathe. The room where decisions actually happen is the one in which it is possible to be honest without making a speech, and noticing which room that is in your own house might tell you something about how your family really talks to one another.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for cumulative observation across multiple homes
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'In every house I have lived in, there has been one room…' và 'What I have noticed in many homes since then is…' dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành để tổng hợp kinh nghiệm trải qua nhiều giai đoạn, không phải một sự kiện cụ thể trong quá khứ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'In every house I have lived in, there has been one room…' and 'What I have noticed in many homes since then is…' use the present perfect to summarise experience across many periods, not a single past event.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for a hypothetical visitor
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If you visited my grandmother's house and tried to guess which corner…, you would almost certainly point to the wrong one' là điều kiện loại 2 — quá khứ đơn ở mệnh đề if, 'would + nguyên thể' ở mệnh đề chính — diễn đạt một tình huống tưởng tượng không thực sự xảy ra.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If you visited my grandmother's house and tried to guess which corner…, you would almost certainly point to the wrong one' is the second conditional — past simple in the if-clause, 'would + base verb' in the main — to describe an imagined situation that is not actually happening.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trong mọi ngôi nhà tôi từng sống, đã luôn có một căn phòng nơi các quyết định thật sự được đưa ra, và trong căn hộ gia đình chúng tôi ở Bangkok căn phòng đó chưa bao giờ là phòng khách trang trọng. Phòng khách là nơi tiếp khách, nơi đặt tivi, nơi treo ảnh cưới của mẹ tôi phía trên ghế sofa. Đó là căn phòng của những lời chào lịch sự và những sinh nhật yên tĩnh. Các quyết định thực sự — về tiền bạc, về việc em trai tôi có nên ra nước ngoài làm việc hay không, về lựa chọn chậm rãi của bố tôi nghỉ hưu sớm hơn hai năm so với kế hoạch — đều đã xảy ra trong bếp, tại một chiếc bàn gỗ nhỏ ngồi được bốn người thoải mái và năm người chỉ với khó khăn. Không ai trong chúng tôi quyết định đây sẽ là quy tắc. Đó là quy tắc bởi vì nhà bếp không tâng bốc ai, và một quyết định được đưa ra trong một căn phòng tâng bốc thường không hẳn là quyết định bạn muốn đưa ra.

Nếu bạn đã thăm nhà bà tôi ở nông thôn và cố đoán xem góc nào đã chứng kiến những cuộc trò chuyện quan trọng nhất của gia đình, bạn gần như chắc chắn sẽ chỉ vào căn phòng sai. Bản năng của khách thăm là tưởng tượng phòng khách trang trọng, với những chiếc ghế thẳng lưng và những bức ảnh người thân trong bộ đồ đẹp nhất. Thực ra, phòng khách là nơi bà phục vụ trà cho người lạ và nơi các anh chị em họ của tôi lặng lẽ xem giờ. Những cuộc trò chuyện thực sự diễn ra ở hiên sau, ngồi trên ghế nhựa, nhìn ra một mảnh vườn rau và một mái tôn, trong khi bà tách đậu vào một cái bát kim loại. Mặc dù phòng khách được thiết kế để trông nghiêm túc, hiên sau là nơi có thể thành thật, một phần vì chỗ ngồi không thoải mái và một phần vì hành động tách đậu cho cả hai người nói điều gì đó để nhìn vào khi cuộc trò chuyện trở nên khó khăn.

Điều tôi đã nhận ra trong nhiều ngôi nhà kể từ đó là gần như luôn có một 'căn phòng quyết định' không phải căn phòng mà một khách thăm sẽ kể tên. Nó có xu hướng hơi không chính thức, hơi bận với một hoạt động nhỏ khác, và hơi khuất. Bàn ăn nơi bài tập về nhà cũng được làm. Quầy bếp nơi ai đó đang cắt rau. Trong xe, trong một chuyến đi dài. Mặc dù các gia đình hiếm khi chọn căn phòng này một cách có ý thức, tôi đã dần nghĩ rằng lựa chọn này có ý nghĩa. Một căn phòng quá trang trọng yêu cầu mọi người phải diễn; một căn phòng quá phân tâm không cho cuộc trò chuyện được thở. Căn phòng mà các quyết định thực sự diễn ra là căn phòng mà bạn có thể thành thật mà không phải đọc một bài diễn văn, và nhận ra căn phòng đó là phòng nào trong ngôi nhà của chính bạn có thể nói cho bạn điều gì đó về cách gia đình bạn thực sự nói chuyện với nhau.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly?
options: In every house I have lived in, there has been one room in which real decisions actually get made.
In every house I have lived in, there has been one room in which real decisions actually gets made.
In every house I lived in, there have been one room in which real decisions actually get made.
In every house I have lived in, there had been one room in which real decisions actually get made.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect with 'one room' (singular) → 'has been'; plural 'decisions' → 'get made'. The first option preserves correct agreement throughout.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly?
options: If you visited my grandmother's house, you would almost certainly point to the wrong one.
If you visit my grandmother's house, you would almost certainly point to the wrong one.
If you visited my grandmother's house, you will almost certainly point to the wrong one.
If you would visit my grandmother's house, you would almost certainly point to the wrong one.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: past simple in the if-clause + 'would + base verb' in the main clause. 'Would' does not appear inside the if-clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly for concession?
options: Although the parlour was designed to look serious, the back porch is the place where it is possible to be honest.
Although the parlour was designed to look serious, but the back porch is the place where it is possible to be honest.
Although, the parlour was designed to look serious the back porch is the place where it is possible to be honest.
The parlour was designed to look serious although however the back porch is the place where it is possible to be honest.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling with 'however'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause with 'in which' correctly?
options: There has been one room in which real decisions actually get made.
There has been one room which in real decisions actually get made.
There has been one room in which real decisions actually getting made.
There has been one room in which real decisions actually got it made.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In which' is the formal relative phrase; word order inside the clause is normal.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'enough' with an adverb correctly?
options: A small wooden table that seats four people comfortably and five only with difficulty.
A small wooden table that seats four people comfortable and five only with difficulty.
A small wooden table that seats four people enough comfortably and five only with difficulty.
A small wooden table that seats four people more comfortably and five only with difficulty.
mcQuestions.explanation: The adverb 'comfortably' modifies the verb 'seats'. The other options use the wrong form or introduce unnecessary comparatives.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'tend to + base verb' correctly?
options: It tends to be slightly informal, slightly busy with another small activity, and slightly out of the way.
It tend to be slightly informal, slightly busy with another small activity, and slightly out of the way.
It tends being slightly informal, slightly busy with another small activity, and slightly out of the way.
It tends to is slightly informal, slightly busy with another small activity, and slightly out of the way.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tend to + base verb' is the standard structure. With singular 'it' the verb is 'tends'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'designed to + base verb' correctly?
options: The parlour was designed to look serious.
The parlour was designed to looking serious.
The parlour was designed for look serious.
The parlour was designed for looking serious.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Be designed to + base verb' expresses purpose. 'Designed for + -ing' is less natural for an action purpose; 'designed for look' is wrong.

options: Looking out at a vegetable patch and a tin roof, while she shells beans into a metal bowl.
Looking out at a vegetable patch and a tin roof, while she shelling beans into a metal bowl.
Looking out at a vegetable patch and a tin roof, while she shell beans into a metal bowl.
Looking out at a vegetable patch and a tin roof, while she has been shells beans into a metal bowl.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'While + subject + verb' is grammatical; 'she shells' (third person singular present). 'She shelling' lacks an auxiliary; the rest are incorrect.

options: A room that is too formal asks people to perform.
A room that is to formal asks people to perform.
A room that is too much formal asks people to perform.
A room that is too formally asks people to perform.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Too + adjective' is the standard pattern. 'To' (not 'too') is a different word; 'too much + adjective' is wrong; 'formally' is an adverb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'come to + base verb' correctly for a gradual conclusion?
options: I have come to think the choice is significant.
I have come thinking the choice is significant.
I have come to thinking the choice is significant.
I am coming to think the choice is significant.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have come to + base verb' is the standard structure for arriving at a gradual conclusion.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'might tell you something about' correctly?
options: Noticing which room that is in your own house might tell you something about how your family really talks.
Noticing which room that is in your own house might tell to you something about how your family really talks.
Noticing which room that is in your own house might tells you something about how your family really talks.
Noticing which room that is in your own house might to tell you something about how your family really talks.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tell + someone + something' takes a direct object (no preposition); after a modal 'might', use the bare base verb 'tell'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If the room where decisions get made shapes the decisions themselves, would changing the room change the outcomes? Or does the family always find the room it needs, regardless of architecture?
